narrativeText: Rangifer tarandus, a cervid found in the Arctic and subarctic, is called “caribou” in North America and “reindeer” in northern Europe and Asia.
notes: Info panel with photo of live caribou. Photo credit: Ian Gardiner.
Header image for Giraffidae section. Photo credit: Jeff Whitlock. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.
Header image for Bovidae section. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.

narrativeText: Bovids include antelope, goats, sheep, mountain goats, and muskoxen.
notes: Info panel for Bovidae section. Photo credit: Jeff Whitlock.
Photo of common eland. Photo credit: Jeff Whitlock.

Header image for Ovibos section. Photo credit: Jeff Whitlock. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left. Taxonomy tree includes subfamily Caprinae.

narrativeText: Bison are the largest terrestrial mammals in the Northern Hemisphere. The two remaining species are the American bison and the European bison or wisent (Bison bonasus).
notes: Info panel with photo of American bison skin specimen. Photo credit: David Gilbar. European bison (Bison bonasus) mentioned in narrative text only.

narrativeText: Animal skins can be damaged by light, so curators keep them in cabinets without windows.
notes: Info panel with photo of mountain goat skin specimen. Photo credit: David Gilbar.

narrativeText: Antelopes, which include the genus Oryx, are fast runners thanks to their long, powerful legs. Their speed and excellent endurance help them evade predators.
notes: Info panel with photo of scimitar oryx. Photo credit: Jeff Whitlock.
Header image for Ovis section. Photo credit: Jeff Whitlock. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.
Photo of Stone sheep (ewe with lamb). No photo credit visible.

narrativeText: The horns of a male bighorn sheep can weigh up to 14 kilograms. The female’s horns are significantly smaller.
notes: Info panel with photo of a bighorn sheep lying down. Photo credit: Jeff Whitlock. Species label reads 'Bighorn sheep' / 'Ovis canadensis'.
Mostly dark/black image with a small gray rectangle in the upper left corner and an orange-brown strip along the bottom edge. No readable text.

Info panel with photo of killer whale spy-hopping. Photo credit: Jackie Hildering.

narrativeText: Whales, dolphins, and porpoises are mammals that have returned to the water. They adapted to the life aquatic thanks to the loss of their external back legs, the evolution of their front legs into flippers, and the development of blowholes on the tops of their heads.
notes: Header-style image for cetacean section. Photo of live Dall's porpoise. Photo credit: Jackie Hildering.
Photo of Hubbs' beaked whale skull specimen. Label reads 'Hubbs' beaked whale (skull)'. Photo credit: David Gilbar.
Header panel. Photo credit: Brian Klinkenberg. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible on left side.

narrativeText: Horses have evolved over millions of years from multi-toed mammals to the single-toed animals we know today. Most horses are domesticated; the few remaining wild populations are endangered.
notes: Info panel continuing Equidae section. Photo of Mountain zebra present.
Dual-family header panel. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible on left side. Photo of Great Indian rhinoceros.

narrativeText: Caribou and other deer, like all members of the order Artiodactyla, are among the mammals with an even number of toes on each foot.
notes: Photo of a Fallow deer specimen/model, credited to David Shackleton. Narrative text about Artiodactyla toe count.

narrativeText: As white-tailed deer adapted to human settlements, they also expanded their range. In some areas they are considered an agricultural pest for consuming commercial crops.
notes: Photo credit: Jeff Whitlock. Photo shows a white-tailed deer looking directly at camera in a grassy/reedy setting.

narrativeText: Members of the dog family, called canids, usually have long legs, bushy tails, and clawed feet. Canids are found on every continent except Antarctica.
notes: Info panel with coyote photo (credit: Brian Klinkenberg) and narrative text about canids.

narrativeText: Although grey wolves are fast runners, they are better known for their endurance. Slightly webbed toes allow them to move easily over snow, and scent glands between the toes leave chemical markers behind to inform other wolves of their whereabouts.
notes: Info panel with black wolf photo (credit: Ian Gardiner). Label reads 'Canis lupis' as printed.
Mostly black image with small grey rectangle in upper left and brown/red strip along bottom. Same pattern as Face04_01. No visible text or label content.
Photo panel of red fox (credit: Jamy Lee). No narrative text visible, only species label.

narrativeText: Most bears are omnivorous, feeding on both plants and animals. The polar bear (Ursus maritimus), however, is entirely carnivorous, while the giant panda (Ailuropoda melanoleuca) eats mainly bamboo.
notes: Photo of black bear credited to Brian Klinkenberg. Part of bear information series on Face04.

narrativeText: Bears rely mainly on their sense of smell to find food. Their sense of smell is one of the best among mammals.
notes: Photo of grizzly bear credited to Ian Gardiner.
Blank dark panel with no text or specimen content. Small gray rectangle in upper left corner and brown/rust strip along bottom edge.

narrativeText: The weasel family is made up of small, carnivorous mammals with short legs, small ears, and thick fur.
notes: Photo of American badger by David Shackleton. Narrative text about the weasel family.
Dark/blank panel with no visible text content. Brown/rust-colored strip along the bottom edge.

narrativeText: Before spotted skunks let loose their smelly, defensive spray, they warn their enemies by stamping their front paws, then turning around and standing on their front legs with their tails and hind legs in the air.
notes: Photo of Western spotted skunk specimen by David Gilbar. Label reads 'Western spotted skunk (specimen) / Spilogale gracilis'.
Dark/black image with a small gray rectangle in upper left and a brown/red strip along the bottom. No visible text.

Header image for Caudata (Salamanders) and Gymnophiona (Caecilians) section. Photo of Northwestern salamander on yellow leaf. Photo credit: Virgil Hawkes. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left. Taxonomy tree shows Tetrapoda > Amphibia > Caudata / Gymnophiona.
Header image for Anura (Frogs) section. Photo of Pacific tree frog. Photo credit: Virgil Hawkes. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.

narrativeText: Many amphibians, which include salamanders, caecilians, and frogs, live in water as juveniles and move onto land as adults.
notes: Info panel about amphibians. Photo of Pacific treefrog on pink flower. Photo credit: Minette Layne. Species label reads 'Pacific treefrog' / 'Pseudacris regilla'.

Header image for Testudines (Turtles) and Crocodilia (Crocodiles) section. Photo of green sea turtle. Photo credit: Ryan Cloutier. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left. Taxonomy tree shows Tetrapoda > Amniota > Reptilia > Testudines and Diapsida > Archosauromorpha > Crocodilia.
Header image for Lacertilia (Lizards) section. Photo of Moorish wall gecko. Photo credit: Gennifer Meldrum. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.

narrativeText: Like many other reptiles, lizards have scales and are “cold-blooded,” or ectothermic. Their blood isn’t actually cold, but the animals’ body temperatures depend on the temperature of their environments. That’s why lizards like to sunbathe on rocks.
notes: Info panel about lizards and ectothermy. Photo of Komodo dragon. Photo credit: Gerrit Velema. Species label reads 'Komodo dragon' / 'Varanus komodoensis'.
Header image for Ochotonidae (Pikas) section. Photo of American pika. Photo credit: Nigel Tate. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.
Header image for Leporidae (Rabbits and hares) section. Photo of Black-tailed jackrabbit. Photo credit: Mike Baird. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.

narrativeText: Full-grown rabbits and hares look quite similar, but they’re very different at birth. Rabbits are born altricial, meaning they’re naked with closed eyes. Hares are precocial at birth, with fur and open eyes.
notes: Info panel about rabbits vs hares. Photo of European rabbit. Species label reads 'European rabbit' / 'Oryctolagus cuniculus'. No photo credit visible.
Header image for Chiroptera (Bats) section. Photo of Large flying fox hanging from branch. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left. No photo credit visible.
Header image for Felidae section. Photo of Canadian lynx. Photo credit: Art Goldenberg. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.

narrativeText: Members of the cat family are carnivorous mammals — the strictest carnivores, in fact, in the order Carnivora. Retractable claws and good night vision contribute to the hunting prowess of felids.
notes: Info panel about Felidae. Photo of Cougar on rock. Photo credit: David Shackleton. Species label reads 'Cougar' / 'Puma concolor'.

Header image for Hyaenidae (Hyenas) and Herpestidae (Mongooses and civets) section. Photo of Meerkat standing upright. Species label reads 'Meerkat' / 'Suricata suricatta'. 'Inside the cabinet' text visible on left.
Mostly dark/black image with a small gray rectangle in the upper left corner and an orange-brown strip along the bottom edge. No readable text.
Header panel. Photo by Jeff Whitlock. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'.

narrativeText: Rodents account for about 40 per cent of mammal species. Since their front teeth never stop growing, rodents must use them constantly to wear them down.
notes: Info panel with beaver photo by Steve Urszenyi.

commonGroupName: Voles, New World rats and mice
notes: Header panel. Photo by Brian Klinkenberg. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'.

commonGroupName: Voles, New World rats and mice
narrativeText: Voles, subfamily Arvicolinae, look similar to mice, but their bodies are more compact. In North America, voles are sometimes called field mice.
notes: Info panel with vole photo by David Shackleton.

commonGroupName: Voles, New World rats and mice
narrativeText: Many adaptations help the muskrat lead a semi-aquatic life. These include semi-webbed feet, long tails that are flattened side-to-side and help them swim, and ears that close to keep water out.
notes: Info panel with muskrat photo by Eileen Brown.

commonGroupName: Voles, New World rats and mice
narrativeText: Mice are small rodents with large ears and long tails. Thanks to human introductions, the house mouse is now found throughout the world.
notes: Info panel with mouse photo by Jeff Whitlock.

Header panel for Serpentes. Photo credit: Virgil Hawkes.

narrativeText: Snakes are reptiles without legs, eyelids, or external ears. Nine species of snakes, including the common garter snake, are native to British Columbia.
notes: Info panel about snakes. Photo credit: Virgil Hawkes.

narrativeText: Boas kill their prey by squeezing them until they suffocate. The tail of British Columbia’s native boa, the rubber boa, looks just like its head!
notes: Info panel about boas. Photo credit: Kristiina Ovaska.
Header panel for Mammalia. Photo of elk fetus in specimen jar. Photo credit: David Gilbar.
Header panel for Aves. Photo of white wagtail specimen in jar. Photo credit: David Gilbar.

narrativeText: The specimens in these cabinets are stored in alcohol. Alcohol can preserve internal organs and other soft tissues that can’t be preserved in dry specimens.
notes: Info panel about alcohol preservation. Photo of chicken fetus in specimen jar. Photo credit: David Gilbar.

Photo of a male Mallard duck on grass. Photo credit: Gracey Stinson. Caption at lower right reads Mallard, Anas platyrhynchos.

narrativeText: Ducks are often classified based on how and what they eat. Diving ducks search for fish and plants underwater, while dabbling ducks either skim plants off the surface or dip their heads into the water, tipping their tail feathers in the air.
notes: Photo of a female Mallard duck close-up. Caption reads Mallard, Anas platyrhynchos. Photo credit: Jennifer Ellis. Narrative text about duck feeding classification.

narrativeText: The diverse primate order includes lemurs, lorises, monkeys, apes, and humans. Known for their large brains and opposable thumbs, primates are one of the few orders of mammals that rely on vision, rather than smell, as their principal sense.
notes: Info panel continuing Primates section. Photo of Guinea baboon with narrative text.
Header panel for Sciuridae / Squirrels and marmots section. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet' vertically. Photo of eastern grey squirrel with label.

narrativeText: Marmots, genus Marmota, are large ground squirrels that live in mountainous areas of North America, Europe, and Asia. Here on the West Coast, the Vancouver Island marmot is classified as endangered by climate change, clear-cut logging, and predators.
notes: Info panel continuing Sciuridae section. Photo of Vancouver Island marmot by David Nagorsen with narrative text.
Photo panel showing Arctic ground squirrels. Photo credit: Charles Krebs.
Photo panel showing a Columbian ground squirrel standing upright on a rock.

notes: Header panel for Gaviiformes / Loons. Photo of Red-throated loon (Gavia stellata) with caption. Taxonomy tree on upper right.

narrativeText: Thanks to its heavy bones, taking flight is no easy feat for the loon. On calm days, it can run as far as several hundred metres along the water's surface before gaining enough speed for take-off.
notes: Info panel with photo of Common loon juvenile in flight. Photo credit: Jed Sheehan. Caption reads 'Common loon (juvenile) / Gavia immer'.

narrativeText: Migration doesn’t always mean flying south for the winter. Many grouse have “altitudinal migrations,” spending their summers in high alpine meadows and winters at low elevations. The blue grouse, however, does just the opposite — a “reverse migration.”
notes: Info panel with photo of Blue grouse (Dendragapus obscurus). Photo credit: Patrick Gaines. Caption reads 'Blue grouse / Dendragapus obscurus'.

narrativeText: Not all ducks nest on the ground — or even near it! Wood ducks lay their eggs in hollow tree trunks, up to 15 metres above the ground. Upon hatching, the newborns have no choice but to jump to the ground.
notes: Photo of wood duck eggs in a nest. Caption reads 'Wood duck (eggs)' and 'Aix sponsa'. Photo credit: Kristian Sven. Narrative text about wood duck nesting behavior.

narrativeText: From 1950 to 1970, peregrine falcon populations in Europe and North America crashed due to pesticide poisoning. These birds are now protected in most parts of Canada and the United States, as well as in many other parts of the world.
notes: Photo of a peregrine falcon with caption. Photo credit: Ingrid Taylar.

narrativeText: The order Passeriformes accounts for more than half of all bird species and includes swallows, sparrows, thrushes, wood warblers, and many, many more.

Photo panel with Gray jay. Photo credit: Nigel Tate.

narrativeText: The crow family, Corvidae, includes some of the smartest birds in the world. Ravens mimic the calls of other birds, and captive blue jays have even been known to use tools to gather food!
notes: Info panel featuring a Carrion crow egg photo and narrative about Corvidae intelligence. Photo credit: David Gilbar.
Photo of a Steller's jay perched on a branch. Photo credit: Nigel Tate.

narrativeText: Woodpeckers have several traits that set them apart from other bird families, including very long, barbed tongues covered with glue-like saliva. They use these sticky tongues to search tree crevices for unsuspecting insects.
notes: Info panel with narrative text about woodpecker tongues. Photo of Red-breasted sapsucker, credit Kevin Cole.

narrativeText: The spotted owl is one of the most endangered owls in the world. In Canada, it's only found in southwestern British Columbia, where only eight pairs are still alive in the wild. The spotted owl relies on old-growth forests, few of which still exist in North America.
notes: Photo credit: Karen Hollingsworth. Large photograph of a Northern spotted owl with narrative text panel.

narrativeText: The arctic tern boasts one of the most impressive migrations of all birds. Every year, it flies 70,000 kilometres, from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back! Not bad for a bird that weighs just over 100 grams!
notes: Photo credit: Sam Judson. Large photograph of an Arctic tern in flight with narrative text panel.
Photo credit: David Gilbar. Label reads 'Common murre (egg)' with scientific name Uria aalge. Shows a single pear-shaped egg with brown markings on white shell.

Header panel for Sponges section. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Erika Paradis. Caption: 'Decorated warbonnet, in cloud sponge'.

narrativeText: Sponges have many different kinds of cells that perform different functions -- some feed, while others protect or reproduce. Unlike other animals, however, sponges do not have true tissues or organ systems.
notes: Info panel for Sponges. Photo credit: Jim Patterson. Caption: 'Dead man's finger, Neoesperiopsis sp.'
Header panel for Corals section. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Commander William Harrigan.

narrativeText: A coral is really a “city” of millions of small animals, called polyps, building new layers on top of the skeletons of their ancestors. They’re genetically identical, and they’re so integrated that some scientists say a coral could be called a “superorganism.”
notes: Info panel for Corals. Photo credit: Albert Kok. Caption: 'Table coral, Acropora pulchra'.

species.commonName: Red and white Christmas tree worm

notes: Header panel for Tube worms section. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Nick Hobgood.

narrativeText: We don’t normally think of worms as beautiful, but tube worms are indisputably gorgeous. These brightly coloured creatures show off their elaborate headpieces while filter-feeding on the ocean floor.
notes: Info panel for Tube worms. Photo credit: John Rix. Caption: 'Duster worm, Eudistylia vancouveri'.

notes: Header panel for Chitons section. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Colin Bates.

narrativeText: Chitons can usually be found grazing on algae in the ocean's intertidal zone—the area that's underwater at high tide and exposed to air at low tide. The world's largest chiton, the gumboot chiton, is commonly found in British Columbia.
notes: Photo credit: Ron Wolf. Photo shows a gumboot chiton in situ among purple sea urchins.

notes: Header panel for Gastropoda section. Photo shows a nudibranch (Nudibranchia) on rock. Photo credit: Ryan Cloutier.

narrativeText: Very early in their lives, gastropods go through a process called torsion, during which the animal twists on itself in such a way that its anus ends up over its head. Torsion allows gastropods to tuck their heads into the opening of their coiled shells for protection.
notes: Photo credit: Andy Goryachev. Photo shows two banana slugs.
Photo of tiger slug on black background. Photo credit: Dr. Arthur Anker. No narrative text on this panel.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Mollusca > Gastropoda > Conidae
notes: Header panel for Gastropoda: Conidae section. Photo shows geography cone snails. Photo credit: Ryan Cloutier.

narrativeText: The hard, tongue-like feeding structure of molluscs, called the radula, comes in many shapes and sizes. The cone snail's poisonous harpoon is actually its radula.
notes: Photo of Dall's cone snail in situ among coral. Photo credit: Olin Feurbacher.
Photo shows six cone snail shells of varying patterns displayed against black background. Label reads 'Cone snails (shells)' with 'Conidae' in italics.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Mollusca > Gastropoda > Cypraeidae
notes: Header panel reading 'Gastropoda: Strombidae' with common name 'Cowries'. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible vertically on left. Bottom right caption reads 'Cowries (shells) Cypraeidae'. Taxonomy tree terminates at Cypraeidae. Multiple cowrie shell photographs displayed. Orange bar at bottom.

narrativeText: In living cowries, the beautiful shell is usually hidden under a layer of textured flesh called the mantle.
notes: Info panel with photograph of living mole cowry showing mantle. Caption reads 'Mole cowry Cypraea talpa'. Photo credit 'Juuyoh Tanaka'. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible vertically on left. Orange bar at bottom.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Mollusca > Gastropoda > Strombidae
notes: Header panel reading 'Gastropoda: Strombidae' with common name 'True conchs'. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible vertically on left. Bottom right caption reads 'True conchs Strombidae'. Taxonomy tree terminates at Strombidae. Three conch shell photographs displayed. Orange bar at bottom.

Info panel with photograph of a living strawberry conch. Caption reads 'Strawberry conch Strombus luhuanus'. Photo credit 'Richard Ling'. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible vertically on left. Orange bar at bottom. No narrative text on this panel.

Header panel reading 'Cnidaria' with common name 'Anemones and jellies'. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible vertically on left. Bottom right caption reads 'Sea anemone Actiniaria'. Photo credit 'Ryan Cloutier'. Taxonomy tree terminates at Cnidaria. Orange bar at bottom.

narrativeText: Cnidarians are jelly-like animals with special cells called nematocysts, which they use to capture prey and protect themselves. A nematocyst fires a tiny, harpoon-like thread, which often contains poison to paralyze the cnidarian’s victim.
notes: Info panel with photograph of lion's mane jellyfish. Caption reads 'Lion's mane jellyfish Cyanea capillata'. Photo credit 'Travis Shinabarger'. Orange bar at bottom.

Header panel reading 'Ctenophora' with common name 'Cone jellies'. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible vertically on left. Bottom right caption reads 'Comb jellyfish Ctenophora'. Photo credit 'The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration'. Taxonomy tree terminates at Ctenophora. Orange bar at bottom.
Header panel for Platyhelminthes (Flatworms). Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo of Divided flatworm credited to Richard Ling. Taxonomy tree displayed in upper right.

narrativeText: The highly flattened bodies of flatworms increase surface area to aid breathing and movement. The group contains some of the most striking animals on Earth, from grotesque parasitic tapeworms to beautiful, free-living tropical flatworms.
notes: Info panel for Flatworms. Photo of Persian carpet flatworm credited to Richard Ling.
Photo panel showing a tapeworm (Cestoda sp.). Photo credited to Ondrej Zicha. No narrative text on this panel.
Header panel for Nemertea (Ribbon worms). Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo of ribbon worm credited to Dr. Arthur Anker. Taxonomy tree displayed in upper right.

narrativeText: Most ribbon worms are very small, but some species can grow up to 30 metres — making them the longest animals in the world! To feed, a ribbon worm shoots a long appendage, called a proboscis, out of its head and wraps it around its prey.
notes: Info panel for Ribbon worms. Photo of Spoon worm (Baseodiscus hemprichii) credited to Dr. Arthur Anker.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Annelida
notes: Header panel. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Sheila Byers.

narrativeText: The annelids include a huge variety of marine segmented worms, as well as land-dwelling species like earthworms and leeches. Although leeches are infamous parasites with powerful suckers, they also contain medically important molecules that stop blood clotting and dilate blood vessels.
notes: Info panel for Annelida. Label beneath image reads 'Bloodworm / Glyceridae'. Photo credit: Sheila Byers.

narrativeText: The bone-eating snot-flower worm feasts on decaying whale bones, part of a whole ecosystem that a whale carcass can support on the ocean floor. Half the worm stays buried inside the bone, and the other half emerges as a pink, flowerlike plume covered in—you guessed it—snot.
notes: Info panel for Annelida continued. Photo credit: Adrian Glover.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Ecdysozoa > Arthropoda > Crustacea
notes: Header panel. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Ryan Cloutier.

species.commonName: Tropical hermit crab inside mollusc shell

narrativeText: Crustaceans, which include lobsters, krill, and barnacles, are the most widely distributed—and some scientists say the most diverse—group of animals on Earth. Most are marine, though a few, such as wood lice or “pill bugs,” live on land.
notes: Info panel for Crustacea. Photo credit: Ryan Cloutier.
Image-only info panel for Crustacea. Label reads 'Woodlice / Porcellio scaber and Oniscus asellus'. No photographer credit visible.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Deuterostomia > Chordata > Urochordata
notes: Header panel. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Kevin King.

narrativeText: A close relative of vertebrates, tunicates (or sea squirts) begin life as free-swimming tadpoles. Eventually, the tadpole settles onto a suitable rock and glues itself to it forever. The adult tunicate grows a thick covering to protect itself from predators and a basket-like organ for filter-feeding.
notes: Photo credit: Richard Ling. Image shows yellow/orange sea tulips. Grey rectangle in upper left corner (likely a map/locator graphic).
Header panel for Echinodermata section. Text 'Inside the cabinet' displayed vertically on left. Photo credit: Colin Bates. Grey rectangle in upper left corner.
Photo panel showing dense cluster of ochre stars in purple and orange. Photo credit: Colin Bates. Grey rectangle in upper left corner.

narrativeText: A sea cucumber can distract predators by gracefully squirting its guts and respiratory organs out through its anus (a process called evisceration). Once it has escaped danger, it can start to rebuild its digestive system.
notes: Photo credit: Erika Paradis. Close-up image of red sea cucumber. Grey rectangle in upper left corner.
Photo panel showing red feather star. Photo credit: Richard Ling. Grey rectangle in upper left corner.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Mollusca > Bivalva
notes: Header panel for Bivalvia section. Text 'Inside the cabinet' displayed vertically on left. Photo credit: Colin Bates. Taxonomy tree ends with 'Bivalva' as displayed. Grey rectangle in upper left corner.

narrativeText: When it comes to avoiding predators, bivalves have two main strategies. Infaunal bivalves bury themselves in the sand using a very strong muscular "foot." Epifaunal bivalves, meanwhile, attach themselves to rocks and can develop thick shells with strong spines.
notes: Photo caption reads 'Mussels attached to rocks' with label 'Bivalvia'. Photo credit: Colin Bates. Image shows mussels on rocks/sand. Grey rectangle in upper left corner.
Photo of a giant lion's paw scallop shell on black background. Photo credit: Randolph Femmer. Orange bar at bottom.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Mollusca > Bivalva > Veneroida
notes: Header panel for Bivalva: Veneroida section. Title reads 'Bivalva: Veneroida' with subtitle 'Clams and cockles'. Sidebar text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo of a common cockle shell. Photo credit: Feron Benjamin. Taxonomy tree with Veneroida highlighted in yellow. Orange bar at bottom.
Photo of a giant clam in underwater reef setting. Photo credit: Ryan Cloutier. Orange bar at bottom.

narrativeText: A local BC bivalve, the geoduck is the largest burrowing clam in the world, famous for digging very deep into the sand. It is also one of the most long-lived animals on Earth, with a life expectancy of about 150 years.
notes: Photo of a Pacific geoduck specimen on black background. Photo credit: Russell Docksteader. Orange bar at bottom.

Left portion of a two-part header panel for Cephalopoda section. Visible text: 'Cephalopoda' and 'Squids, octopuses, and'. Sidebar text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Title is cut off at right edge. Orange bar at bottom.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Mollusca > Cephalopoda
notes: Right portion of a two-part header panel for Cephalopoda section. Visible text: 'nautiluses'. Photo of a reef octopus. Photo credit: John Rix. Taxonomy tree with Cephalopoda highlighted in bold. Orange bar at bottom.

narrativeText: Cephalopods have an amazing ability to change colour and texture. This helps them not only camouflage themselves to avoid predators, but also show aggression or attract a mate.
notes: Photo of a reef cuttlefish in underwater setting. Photo credit: Nick Hobgood. Orange bar at bottom.
Photo credit: Lee Berger. Image shows a living nautilus with striped shell and tentacles visible.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Mollusca > Gastropoda
notes: Header panel for Gastropoda section. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Colin Bates.
Two photographs of the same species shown from different angles. Photo credit: Ryan Cloutier.

taxonomyTree: Animalia > Eumetazoa > Bilateria > Protostomia > Lophotrochozoa > Mollusca > Gastropoda > Volutidae
notes: Header panel for Gastropoda: Volutidae section. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Ria Tan.

narrativeText: Volutes are predatory sea snails with great appetites for other molluscs and echinoderms. They use their tongue-like radulas to penetrate openings in the shells of their prey and eat them from the inside out.
notes: Info panel for Volutes section. Photo credit: Richard Ling.

Header panel. Title reads 'Rhodophyta: Corallinales / Coralline algae'. Sidebar text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo captioned 'Coralline rhodolith' credited to Artiom Shostak. Taxonomy tree displayed top-right.
Photo panel showing marine scene. Caption reads 'Graceful coral seaweed / Corallina vancouveriensis'. Photo credit: Colin Bates. No narrative text beyond caption.

narrativeText: Many coralline algae form crusts on rock surfaces. Each year some grow a new layer, about a third of a millimetre thick. Scientists in Alaska have found crusts that are 20 centimetres thick, estimated to be more than 700 years old!
notes: Photo of encrusting coralline algae on rock. Caption reads 'Rugose pink rock crust / Lithothamnion phymatodeum'. Photo credit: Colin Bates.

narrativeText: Researchers study coralline algal crusts like they do tree rings, to infer past global climate conditions such as ocean temperatures hundreds of years ago.
notes: Photo of Bossiella sp. growing on encrusting coralline. Caption reads 'Bossiella sp. and Rugose pink rock crust, Lithothamnion phymatodeum'. Photo credit: Colin Bates.
Blank dark panel with no visible text or specimen content.
Specimen photo showing multiple branching coralline algae. Caption reads 'Tenarea dispar on Macmillan's coral seaweed, Serraticardia macmillanii'. Photo credit: Artiom Shostak.

narrativeText: Corallines are not your typical red algae. Unlike other Rhodophyta, every single cell of a coralline alga is firmly encased in calcium carbonate, making them the hardest plants on the planet!
notes: Photo of branching red coralline algae. Caption reads 'Bead coral seaweed / Calliarthron tuberculosum'. Photo credit: Colin Bates.

Label reads 'Mesophyllum vancouveriense (on mussel)'. Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Gray rectangle in upper left (possibly obscured label or adjacent panel edge). Two mussels shown with encrusting pink/white coralline algae.
Three species listed on a single label. Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Two rock specimens shown with encrusting algae in green and pink/purple. Gray rectangle in upper left.

Header panel for Cyanobacteria section. Circular microscope image of Gloeotrichia echinulata, credited to Kristian Peters. Text 'Inside the cabinet' displayed vertically on left side. Gray rectangle in upper left.

narrativeText: Constantinea is one of the longest-living fleshy algae known. Scientists have found specimens nearly 50 years old, but most only live 15 years or less.
notes: Specimen photograph of a red, perforated, leaf-like alga. Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Narrative text displayed alongside specimen. Gray rectangle in upper left.
Second specimen photograph of Constantinea subulifera, showing red leaf-like fronds with dark holdfast region. Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Gray rectangle in upper left.

narrativeText: Gelidium is a source of agar, which is often used to thicken or stabilize foods and industrial products. Scientists use it to grow bacteria and fungi, and you'll find it in some diet foods and desserts!
notes: Shows photograph of turf red algae (Gelidium pusillum) credited to D.S. Littler, and a circular inset photograph of agar credited to Unkky Panacek. Gray rectangle in upper left corner (likely display artifact or adjacent panel edge).
Dark/black panel with no visible text or specimen content. Gray rectangle in upper left corner. Green and blue horizontal bands at bottom edge.

Dark/black panel with no visible text or specimen content. Gray rectangle in upper left corner. Green and blue horizontal bands at bottom edge.
Large photograph of iridescent red seaweed (Mazzaella sp.) credited to Colin Bates. Gray rectangle in upper left corner.

narrativeText: Mastocarpus is heteromorphic, meaning it grows as different forms in different phases of its life cycle. In its gametophyte phase it is erect and blade-like. In its sporophyte phase, it is a crust that looks like tar spots on rocks.
notes: Photograph shows Petrocelis (labeled as 'crustose phase of Mastocarpus') on rocks, credited to Patrick Martone. Gray rectangle in upper left corner.
Dark/black panel with no visible text or specimen content. Gray rectangle in upper left corner. Green and blue horizontal bands at bottom edge.

narrativeText: Mazzaella contains carrageenan, which you'll find on the ingredient list of many products, including chocolate milk, ice cream, some cheeses, and toothpaste. Carrageenan thickens and stabilizes.
notes: Photo credit: Patrick Martone. Photo of seaweed in natural habitat with caption identifying species.
Dark/blank panel with no visible text or content. Blue and green horizontal bars at bottom edge.
Photo credit: Colin Bates. Close-up photograph of a red/coral-colored branching seaweed specimen. Scientific name caption visible below photo.
Two photographs of sea sac seaweed. Left photo credit: Patrick Martone. Right photo credit: Colin Bates. Shows yellow-golden sac-like structures.

narrativeText: In Japan, China, and Korea, some species of Porphyra are cultivated on nets to produce nori, a popular edible seaweed. Some BC First Nations also eat local Porphyra species, which the Squamish Salish call “lhék’es.”
notes: Photo credit: Patrick Martone. Shows leafy green seaweed specimens, possibly dried/preserved, with mussels visible in background.

narrativeText: Porphyra is heteromorphic, with a bladed or leafy phase alternating with a microscopic, filamentous phase that bores into barnacles and clamshells.
notes: Photo credit: Patrick Martone. Close-up of a single leafy Porphyra specimen. Continues the Porphyra/Nori information from the previous panel.
Dark/blank panel with no visible text or content. Blue and green horizontal bars at bottom edge.

narrativeText: Inside the cabinet
notes: Header panel for Rhodophyta / Red algae section. Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Specimen image shows pink-red pressed algae fronds.

narrativeText: Many algal genera are named after people who studied algae. Can you guess the names of the people whom the following genera were named after: Smithora? Turnerella? Weeksia? Zinovaea?
notes: Info panel with interactive question about algal genus naming. Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Specimen shows pink/purple pressed algae. Genera referenced: Smithora, Turnerella, Weeksia, Zinovaea.

narrativeText: The genus Smithora is named for G.M. Smith, author of Marine Algae of the Monterey Peninsula, Turnerella for D. Turner, an early 19th century British banker and phycologist, Weeksia for Mrs. J.M. Weeks, a California seaweed enthusiast in the late 19th century, and Zinovaea for A.D. Zinova, a Russian phycologist of the mid-20th century.
notes: Answer panel to the question posed in Face14_02. Photo credit: Patrick Martone. Specimen shows red algae close-up. Genera referenced: Smithora, Turnerella, Weeksia, Zinovaea.
Blank black panel with no visible text or specimen content.

notes: Header panel for Chlorophyta / Green algae section. Photo credit: Patrick Martone. Image shows forked green algae specimen.
Specimen photo panel showing green algae (Ulva) growing on rocks. Photo credit: Colin Bates.
Specimen photo panel showing underwater scene with green algae (Ulva) and fish. Photo credit: Colin Bates.

narrativeText: Green algae (phylum Chlorophyta) get their name from their green photosynthetic pigment, chlorophyll. Red and brown algae also have chlorophyll, but they have other pigments that hide its colour.
notes: Microscope photograph at 100x magnification. Photo credit: Linden Gledhill.
Photo credit: Colin Bates.
Includes macro photograph and 100x microscope inset. Photo credit: Patrick Martone.
Header panel for Ochrophyta / Brown algae section. Text 'Inside the cabinet' visible on left. Photo credit: Colin Bates.
Photo credit: Colin Bates.

Photo credit: Colin Bates.

narrativeText: Desmarestia cells contain sulfuric acid, which scientists think might be a chemical defense against predators like sea urchins.
notes: Photo credit: Patrick Mortane. Image shows close-up photograph of Desmarestia ligulata with magnified inset of branch detail.
Photo credit: Aika Yamaguchi. Microscope image at 100x magnification showing circular diatom.

narrativeText: Diatoms live almost everywhere that's wet, from the oceans to damp surfaces and from the poles to the tropics. The diatom Pseudo-nitzschia produces domoic acid, which is concentrated by shellfish and can cause gastrointestinal and neurological disorders in humans who eat them.
notes: Photo credit: Naoji Yubuki. Electron microscope image at 2,800x magnification with 10 um scale bar.
Dark/blank panel with no visible text or specimen content. Small gray rectangle in upper-left corner and green/blue stripe along bottom edge.

narrativeText: There could be up to 200,000 species of diatom, about a tenth of which have been described. Their silica cell walls are beautifully ornamented with many small holes. Fossil deposits of these hard silica shells, known as diatomaceous earth, are mined today for use in everything from dynamite to insecticides.
notes: Photo credit: Naoji Yubuki. Electron microscope image at 400x magnification with 5 um scale bar. Shows cylindrical diatom frustule.

Header panel for Rhodophyta: Corallinales section. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Caption reads 'Lithophyllum sp. on Bleachweed, Prionitis lanceolata'. Photo shows coralline algae encrusting on bleachweed branches.
Dark/blank panel with no visible text or specimen content. Small gray rectangle in upper-left corner and green/blue stripe along bottom edge.

narrativeText: Coralline algae live in every ocean on Earth, from warm Caribbean reefs to cold Alaskan waters. Corallines colonize bare rock, hold coral reefs together, and provide important habitat for many reef organisms.
notes: Photo credit: Colin Bates. Photo shows bead coral seaweed and California purple sea urchin.
Dark panel with no visible text or specimen labels. Blue and green horizontal bars at bottom.

narrativeText: When carbon dioxide accumulates in the atmosphere, some of it dissolves into the ocean, raising its acidity (decreasing its pH). In low-pH conditions, coralline algae can’t calcify and therefore can’t survive.
notes: Photo credit: Colin Bates. Photo caption identifies Bossiella sp.
Dark panel with no visible text or specimen labels. Blue and green horizontal bars at bottom.
Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Specimen photograph with label identifying Graceful coral seaweed, Corallina vancouveriensis.

narrativeText: Coralline algae don’t appeal to herbivores — each bite is like a mouthful of rock! But corallines depend on herbivores to keep their surfaces clean of other algae and scum.
notes: Photo credit: Colin Bates. Photo shows Rugose pink rock crust (Lithothamnion phymatodeum) and Swirled pink rock crust (Pseudolithophyllum whidbeyense).
Photo credit: Colin Bates. Close-up specimen photograph of Bead coral seaweed, Calliarthron tuberculosum.

Header panel. Title reads 'Rhodophyta: Corallinales / Coralline algae'. Left side has 'Inside the cabinet' label. Photo caption: 'Graceful coral seaweed Corallina vancouveriensis and Bossiella sp.' Credit: Colin Bates. Taxonomy tree in upper right.

narrativeText: Some corallines have joints called genicula, made of soft tissues. Genicula are stronger, tougher, and stretchier than other fleshy seaweed tissues, and help erect corallines survive under crashing waves.
notes: Info panel with macro photo of Calliarthron cheilosporioides showing joints. Inset labeled 'Genicula 100x' shows microscopic view. Credit: Patrick Martone.

Photo panel showing specimen of Calliarthron cheilosporioides in biomechanical clamps. Caption reads 'Southern bead coral seaweed / Calliarthron cheilosporioides / (genicula in biomechanical clamps)'. Credit: Patrick Martone. Navigation arrows visible at top.
Blank panel. Dark background with green/blue bar at bottom. No text or specimen content visible.

narrativeText: How does a calcified alga reproduce? Like other red algae, corallines can be male, female, or spore-producing sporophytes. In coralline species, these life phases all look similar.
notes: Info panel with two specimens shown. Left: 'Bead coral seaweed / Calliarthron tuberculosum / (male)'. Right: 'Bead coral seaweed / Calliarthron tuberculosum / (sporophyte)'. Credit: Patrick Martone.

narrativeText: Mature coralline fronds produce calcified, volcano-like swellings, called conceptacles, which hold the reproductive parts. These reproductive parts escape through pores in the conceptacle.
notes: Close-up photo panel showing conceptacles on Calliarthron tuberculosum. Caption reads 'Bead coral seaweed / Calliarthron tuberculosum / (showing conceptacles)'. Credit: Patrick Martone.
Blank panel. Dark background with green/blue bar at bottom. No text or specimen content visible.

Two photographs with captions. Left photo shows pink crust on green seagrass blades. Right photo shows branching coral seaweed among dark stems. Both photos credited to Colin Bates. Grey rectangle at far left (likely navigation/map element).

Header panel for Ochrophyta / Brown algae section. Large text reads 'Ochrophyta' and 'Brown algae'. Vertical text along left reads 'Inside the cabinet'. Taxonomy tree in upper right corner. Photo of Sea potato (Leathesia difformis) credited to Colin Bates.
Photograph of a dried bull kelp specimen showing long hollow stipe and bulbous float with blades. Caption reads 'Bull kelp / Nereocystis luetkeana'. Photo credited to Colin Bates.

narrativeText: Bull kelp (Nereocystis) has a long, partially hollow, stem-like structure with a bulbous float on the end. Coastal First Nations used this float to store fish oil and water. Bull kelp can also be used as a trumpet!
notes: Close-up photograph of bull kelp float and blades, with interpretive text. Caption reads 'Bull kelp / Nereocystis luetkeana'. Photo credited to Colin Bates.
Two underwater photographs of bull kelp. Both captioned 'Bull kelp / Nereocystis luetkeana'. Both photos credited to Colin Bates.

narrativeText: Postelsia is a beautiful brown kelp that grows only on the most exposed rocky shores. This seaweed, which resembles a miniature palm tree, reaches half a metre in length at maturity in summer.
notes: Photograph of sea palms growing on rocky shore with interpretive text. Caption reads 'Sea palm / Postelsia palmaeformis'. Photo credited to Patrick Martone.
Close-up photograph of a single sea palm specimen growing on rock among mussels and barnacles. Caption reads 'Sea palm / Postelsia palmaeformis'. Photo credited to Colin Bates.
Mostly black panel with a small gray rectangle in upper-left and blue/green horizontal strips at bottom edge. No readable text or specimen content visible.

narrativeText: Sargassum muticum is a brown seaweed that grows in local waters but isn't native to our coast. It was accidentally introduced with young oysters from Japan when they were first farmed in the Strait of Georgia many decades ago.
notes: Photo of brown seaweed in water credited to David J Glaves. Caption reads 'Japanese seaweed / Sargassum muticum'. Blue/green strips at bottom edge.

Header panel for Oomycetes / Water moulds. Text reads 'Inside the cabinet' vertically. '100x' label near microscope image. Circular microscope photo captioned 'Water mould attacking duckweed / Oomycota' credited to Leo Papandreou. Blue/green strips at bottom.

narrativeText: The bryophytes include three groups of plants: mosses, liverworts, and hornworts. These plants are generally small, have no vascular systems or roots, and reproduce through spores rather than seeds.
notes: Three dried bryophyte specimens displayed with labels. Photo credited to Artiom Shostak. Yellow/green strips at bottom.

narrativeText: You'll find bryophytes in environments ranging from the tundra to the tropics, but they mainly live in moist, shady places. Some, however, grow in deserts, and even underwater.
notes: Photo credit: George Shepherd. Grey rectangle in upper-left corner (obscured or placeholder element).
Photo credit: Ken-ichi Ueda. Grey rectangle in upper-left corner. No narrative text on this panel.
Photo credit: Ken-ichi Ueda. Grey rectangle in upper-left corner. No narrative text on this panel.

narrativeText: Bryophytes have two life stages: the gametophyte stage, which produces the sex organs, and the sporophyte stage, which produces spores. Unlike other plants, however, the bryophyte lives longer in its gametophye stage than its sporophyte stage.
notes: Photo credit: Artiom Shostak. Image has labeled callouts pointing to 'Sporophytes' and 'Gametophytes' on the specimen. Parenthetical on label reads '(dried specimen)'. Note: the panel text contains a likely typo — 'gametophye' instead of 'gametophyte' — transcribed as visible.

narrativeText: Granite moss (Andreaea) lives at high elevations and looks black, due to dark pigment in its cell walls that prevents UV damage.
notes: Info panel with photo of Andreaea rupestris and narrative text. Photo credit: George Shepherd.
Specimen photo panel showing Andreaea nivalis. Photo credit: Derek Tan.

narrativeText: If you were to take a very close look at the “grass” in your yard, you’d likely find that it’s made up of bryophytes as well as grass. Common mosses found in lawns include Pseudoscleropodium purum and Rhytidiadelphus loreus.

narrativeText: Mosses are the most diverse group of bryophytes, with approximately 10,000 species. A moss in its gametophyte stage is green and leafy. In its sporophyte stage, it usually grows short stalks topped with capsules full of spores.

narrativeText: Two to three per cent of the Earth's surface is covered by the moss genus Sphagnum. These mosses are very absorbent—in fact, they can hold up to 20 times their weight in water!

narrativeText: Unlike mosses, hornworts are not leafy. The hornwort’s gametophyte is called a thallus. It looks like a flat sheet, but may be lobed or a bit wrinkled. The hornwort’s spore capsules are tall “horns” that grow out of the thallus.

narrativeText: Liverworts grow in both thallose and leafy forms. You can tell leafy liverworts from mosses because their leaves grow in rows up the stem, while moss leaves grow in spirals. Leafy liverworts have oil bodies (liquid droplets inside their cells), and their sporophytes look different, too.

narrativeText: Spores must be dispersed for new plants to grow. Because the sporophytes of mosses, liverworts, and hornworts vary so much, they release spores in many different ways.
notes: Informational panel about spore dispersal in bryophytes. Two circular magnified photos of Jungermannia sp. sporophyte.

species.notes: Magnified photographs of sporophyte; photo credit George Shepherd

narrativeText: Liverworts' method of dispersing spores differs from other bryophytes. Inside their capsules, spores are attached to cells called elaters, which look like coiled-up springs.
notes: Informational panel about liverwort spore dispersal via elaters. Three circular magnified photos of Jungermannia sp. 'elaters' in italics.

narrativeText: Lycophytes are one of the oldest living groups of vascular plants, arising more than 400 million years ago. Forests of tree-sized lycophytes dominated the landscape during the Carboniferous period. Their modern-day descendants, the club mosses, are much smaller.

narrativeText: Unlike most plants, lycophytes and monilophytes do not have seeds or flowers. Instead, they reproduce by releasing the single-celled spores that grow under their leaves.

narrativeText: Sedges also look similar to grasses, but they have triangular stems. This family includes water chestnut, cotton-grass, and the papyrus sedge, which ancient Egyptians used to make writing paper.

narrativeText: Lichens are fungi and algae or cyanobacteria living in a close relationship, a symbiosis. The algae photosynthesize, making food. In some lichens, the fungus protects the algae from UV radiation. This lifestyle evolved separately many times!

narrativeText: You’ve probably seen “witch’s hair” hanging from old coniferous trees here on the coast, especially in the mountains. You might not have known, however, that witch’s hair is a lichen: Alectoria sarmentosa.

narrativeText: Alectoria sarmentosa was used by several BC First Nations for diapers, bedding, sanitary napkins, and decorations for masks. This lichen is also an important winter food for deer, particularly on Vancouver Island.
notes: Photo credit: Emma Grant. Ethnobotanical/ecological information panel.

narrativeText: A sunflower’s head is actually made up of many tiny flowers, or florets. Florets in the middle of the disk are both male and female, producing pollen and seeds. The yellow rays of the edge florets are sterile and made up of three fused petals.

narrativeText: Budding sunflowers are heliotropic. At sunrise, they face eastward. Throughout the day, they turn westward with the sun, then return to face eastward at night. When sunflowers bloom, they lose this ability to move with the sun.

narrativeText: Many common garden plants are members of the Asteraceae family, including chrysanthemums, gerbera daisies, calendula, dahlias, and zinnias.
The Asteraceae also include many plants we think of as weeds. In fact, 11 of the 21 most noxious weeds in British Columbia, including spotted knapweed and tansy ragwort, are in the Asteraceae.

narrativeText: Lichens are the dominant vegetation on about 8 per cent of the Earth’s terrestrial surface. Sometimes called “nature’s pioneers,” these hardy organisms are often the first plant-like forms to colonize newly exposed areas — even bare rock!

narrativeText: Lichens absorb chemicals in the air and water, making them sensitive to pollution. If a pollutant harms one part of the lichen, the entire organism will die. Because of this, scientists are now using lichens to monitor air quality.

narrativeText: Believe it or not, the Fungal Kingdom's closest relative is the Animal Kingdom. There's a distinct similarity between the zoospores of aquatic fungi and the sperm of animals: each has a tail-like flagellum that propels it forward.

narrativeText: What do you picture when you think about fungi? A mushroom? Mushrooms are reproductive structures formed by some but by no means all fungi. Other fungi reproduce with cups, coral-like stalks, or delicate, microscopic spores called conidia.

narrativeText: Mushrooms reproduce by dispersing spores. Spores are single-celled, often microscopic bodies that get shot out into the space between the gills, then fall downward and are carried by the wind to grow elsewhere.
notes: Info panel with angel wing mushroom photo and narrative text about spore dispersal.

narrativeText: Most Fungi, including those that can produce mushrooms, live most of their lives as masses of microscopic, thread-like hyphae hidden in soil or plant material.

narrativeText: Mycorrhizae are plant roots and fungi living in close partnership. The fungus "fertilizes" the plant with nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, and in return, the plant feeds the fungus with sugars. It's a win-win partnership; in fact, neither grows without the other.
notes: Cross-section diagram showing tree roots and fungal mycorrhizae underground. Inset circular image labeled 'Amanita fungus and pine root mycorrhizae' credited to Randy Molina.

narrativeText: Stinkhorns live up to their name by smelling like rotten meat or dung. Flies attracted to the smell feast on the spores and then disperse them, helping the stinkhorns reproduce.

narrativeText: Some lichens are poisonous. Xanthoparmelia, for example, may have caused the death of 300 elk in Wyoming in 2004. Heavy snowfall meant the lichens were all the elk had to eat — lower doses would probably not have been fatal.

narrativeText: Each newly identified species is given a unique name and is associated with a representative specimen, known as the holotype. Paratypes are specimens of the same species that were used to determine the range of the species' when that species was originally described.

narrativeText: 4.54 billion to 542 million years ago Precambrian
notes: Header panel for Precambrian section. Text on left reads 'Inside the cabinet' (vertical). Credit: David Gilbar. Red bar along bottom.
Black panel with grey rectangle in upper left and red bar along bottom. No text visible.

narrativeText: The Precambrian spans 87 per cent of the Earth’s history. Over these billions of years the Earth saw great changes, including the emergence of cyanobacteria, which released oxygen into the atmosphere through photosynthesis.
notes: Info panel with photo of algae specimen. Credit: David Gilbar. Red bar along bottom.

narrativeText: The oxygen released by cyanobacteria destroyed the methane that was already in the atmosphere. Without it, global temperatures plummeted, causing a glacial period so cold that even the Equator was covered in ice. Scientists call this the “Snowball Earth” hypothesis.
notes: Info panel with photo of stromatolite specimen. Credit: Jens Rydell. Red bar along bottom.

narrativeText: The Devonian is known as the “Age of the Fish” because many new kinds of fish appeared, including the first sharks. Land-dwelling vertebrates also emerged in the Devonian. This period ended after a series of mass extinctions that wiped out as many as three-quarters of species.

narrativeText: Amphibians and insects thrived throughout the Carboniferous—and, in some cases, grew incredibly large. In fact, the largest known flying insect lived during the Carboniferous. Meganeura, as it is now called, looked like a modern dragonfly, except that it had a 75-centimetre wingspan! Very high levels of oxygen in the atmosphere helped insects like Meganeura grow so big.

narrativeText: Reptiles prospered during the Permian. Some pelycosaurs (scaleless reptiles) grew up to three metres long. The Permian ended with “the mother of all extinctions,” which may have killed over 95 per cent of species, including all trilobites as well as major groups of corals, brachiopods, and reptiles.

narrativeText: During the Triassic, all the Earth's continents were stuck together, forming one great supercontinent known as Pangaea. Modern corals appeared in the oceans, and the first dinosaurs emerged on land, along with flying reptiles and the first mammals. Near the end of the Triassic, another mass extinction allowed dinosaurs to become the dominant land vertebrates.

narrativeText: The Cretaceous saw the birth of some of the most famous dinosaurs, including Tyrannosaurus rex and Triceratops. Scientists believe that the impact of an asteroid measuring 10 kilometres across contributed to the mass extinction that killed off the dinosaurs and ended the Cretaceous.

narrativeText: The period leading up to the present saw the appearance of animals we know today, including snakes, dolphins, rodents, and of course the Hominidae, or great apes. Modern humans did not appear until about 250,000 years ago, making us a tiny speck on the fossil record.
